strands.title: System Profile
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat. Four domains: payments, inventory, user auth, order fulfillment. 6-month migration timeline, limited unquantified budget, PCI-DSS hard constraint. Coupling profile is materially worse than initial characterization: payments reaches into inventory, order state, and user profiles (cycle 2), payments DB is confirmed to store auth session tokens (cycle 3, empirical), and payments outage cascaded to auth and caused full application outage last quarter (cycle 4, empirical). That outage is the confirmed trigger for this migration. CEO has issued a hard 90-day constraint for auth independence from payments. Infrastructure details (cloud vs. on-prem, session token TTL) remain unconfirmed — explicitly solicited. Auth ORM coupling nature is now an open question with direct bearing on build-vs-extract decision.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat, four domains, 6-month migration timeline, PCI-DSS constraint
Payments outage cascaded to auth and took down the full application last quarter — empirical, user-stated
CEO has mandated auth independence from payments within 90 days — hard external constraint
Session tokens are stored in the payments DB due to its uptime guarantees — empirical, user-stated
Auth code is coupled to the ORM layer and would need a rewrite anyway — team lead assertion, unverified
Infrastructure (cloud vs. on-prem) and session token TTL are unconfirmed — explicitly solicited

strands.title: Auth Service Design and Build Strategy
strands.content:
New strand integrating cycle 5 greenfield-vs-extraction debate with prior auth design work. Team lead argues for greenfield auth service on grounds that auth code is ORM-coupled and needs a rewrite anyway. Counter-position accepted in part: if ORM coupling is in business logic (auth rules inside JPA entity methods, Hibernate event listeners), greenfield is justified. If coupling is in infrastructure only (shared EntityManager, connection pool), extraction-with-refactor is faster and safer. The diagnostic question is: where does the coupling live?

Recommendation: greenfield is acceptable only under a hard strangler constraint — deletion date for old auth system must be set before new service is written. Two-week maximum parallel operation window. Without this commitment, greenfield becomes indefinite parallel operation, which doubles security surface and creates feature parity drift. Characterization tests against existing auth behavior are required regardless of path — they are the behavior specification for the new service and the test oracle during cutover.

Prior design parameters preserved: Keycloak or Spring Authorization Server, JWT with 15-minute access tokens, longer refresh tokens, monolith delegates during transition. Session token migration (dual-write, shadow-read, TTL drain) applies to both paths — the migration mechanics are independent of whether the new service is extracted or greenfield. Resilience4j circuit breakers remain part of the 90-day delivery.

Decision gate proposed for week 3: after characterization tests are written and ORM coupling is inspected in detail, choose path formally. This prevents premature commitment while maintaining momentum.
depends_on: Auth-Payments DB Entanglement and Session Migration

key_claims.text: Greenfield auth service is acceptable if ORM coupling is in business logic — but requires hard deletion date for old system before build begins
Indefinite parallel operation of two auth systems doubles security surface and creates feature parity drift — primary failure mode of ungoverned greenfield
Characterization tests against existing auth behavior are required regardless of build path
ORM coupling location (business logic vs. infrastructure) is the deciding diagnostic — unconfirmed
Extraction-with-rewrite is faster to cutover than greenfield because behavior is pre-specified by production system
Use Keycloak or Spring Authorization Server — do not build custom token management
Two-week maximum parallel operation window must be enforced if greenfield path is chosen

strands.title: Auth-Payments DB Entanglement and Session Migration
strands.content:
Consolidation of prior Auth-Payments DB Entanglement strand with session migration mechanics. Session tokens in payments DB is empirically confirmed. Creates cyclic extraction dependency: auth extraction needs payments DB access resolved; payments extraction complicated by auth data in its DB. Payments DB is confirmed multi-tenant: carries CDE payment data and auth session state simultaneously — a live PCI scope problem.

Session token migration is critical path regardless of whether auth service is extracted or greenfield. Dual-write strategy: write tokens to both payments DB and new auth DB simultaneously, validate reads from new store in shadow mode, drain old store passively via token TTL expiry, cut reads over only after validation. Short token TTLs (15-minute access tokens if JWT) are a migration lever — natural drain within one TTL window. Token TTL is unconfirmed and explicitly solicited.

Auth DB replacement must match or exceed payments DB uptime SLA — the uptime rationale for original placement is a system requirement, not a historical accident. Managed HA required: RDS Multi-AZ, Cloud Spanner, or Redis Cluster depending on infrastructure (unconfirmed). Payments DB cannot be handed to payment service until session tokens are fully migrated out — hard sequencing constraint that is independent of build-vs-extract decision.

Scope of auth artifacts in payments DB is still unconfirmed — may extend beyond session tokens to credentials, API keys, CSRF tokens.

key_claims.text: Session tokens in payments DB is empirically confirmed — creates hard data-layer dependency between auth and payments extractions
Auth DB must match payments DB uptime SLA — single-instance replacement is not acceptable
Dual-write session token migration is required to avoid mass user logout during cutover
Payments DB cannot be handed to payment service until session tokens are migrated out — hard sequencing constraint
Scope of auth artifacts in payments DB may extend beyond session tokens — unconfirmed

strands.title: 90-Day Auth Independence Target
strands.content:
CEO-mandated hard constraint. 90-day target is achievable but requires precise scoping, dedicated resourcing, and no timeline slippage on dependency mapping. Correct interpretation: auth service extracted or greenfield-built, session tokens on dedicated HA store, payments outage cannot cascade to auth — testable and demoable. Wrong interpretation: full auth + payments separation with clean PCI boundaries — that is 6-month work.

Greenfield debate (cycle 5) does not change the 90-day feasibility assessment materially, but it does affect risk profile: greenfield adds build time and parallel operation risk that extraction avoids. If greenfield path is chosen, the deletion date for old auth must be inside the 90-day window.

Proposed sequence (revised to reflect build-path decision gate): Weeks 1-2 dependency mapping + characterization tests + QSA engagement + auth DB provisioning; Week 3 decision gate on greenfield vs. extraction; Weeks 3-6 session token dual-write migration; Weeks 7-10 auth service build/extraction + Resilience4j circuit breakers; Weeks 11-12 cutover, chaos testing, old auth deletion, validation.

Critical failure modes: (1) ORM coupling inspection skipped — wrong build path chosen without evidence; (2) parallel operation window left open-ended if greenfield chosen; (3) dependency mapping compressed to save time; (4) team splits focus — requires dedicated 2-3 engineer squad; (5) auth DB uptime SLA not resolved week 1; (6) CEO scope mismatch not corrected early.
depends_on: Auth Service Design and Build Strategy
Auth-Payments DB Entanglement and Session Migration

key_claims.text: 90-day auth independence is achievable if scoped correctly and team is protected from context-switching
Greenfield path is compatible with 90-day target only if old auth system deletion date is inside the window
90-day scope must be defined in writing with CEO within first 2 weeks
Cascade failure was a bulkheading problem — Resilience4j circuit breakers are part of the 90-day deliverable
At day 90, payments extraction and full PCI boundary cleanup will not be complete

strands.title: Payment Service Design and PCI-DSS
strands.content:
Third-party card vault (Stripe/Braintree/Adyen) for scope reduction remains primary architectural driver. QSA engagement is now a week 1-2 requirement — framed as emergency scoping confirmation, not full assessment. Session tokens in CDE is a live compliance question predating the migration. Migration framing for QSA: compliance improvement, not risk event.

Payments DB cannot be handed to payment service until session tokens are migrated out — hard sequencing constraint. Network segmentation required from initial deployment. Payment Service API boundary: initiatePayment(OrderId, PaymentToken, Amount), refund(TransactionId, Amount), getStatus(TransactionId). Internal OrderStateUpdate endpoint on monolith remains interim pattern for order state write dependency. Payments extraction is post-90-day-auth-target work.
depends_on: Auth-Payments DB Entanglement and Session Migration

key_claims.text: Third-party card vault dramatically reduces PCI-DSS scope — system should never handle raw PANs
QSA must be engaged at week 1-2 as emergency scoping confirmation with 2-week turnaround expectation
Payments DB cannot be handed to payment service until session tokens are migrated out

strands.title: Coupling and Dependency Analysis
strands.content:
Coupling profile has worsened each cycle through discovery. Confirmed tentacles: payments reaches into inventory (query), order state (write — hardest), user profiles (read), and auth session tokens live in payments DB (bidirectional operational dependency with confirmed blast radius). Auth code is additionally coupled to ORM layer — nature of that coupling (business logic vs. infrastructure) is the new open question from cycle 5.

Pattern of accidental discovery in a 500K LOC codebase means the team's mental model cannot be trusted as the dependency map. jdeps or Structure101 analysis remains a hard prerequisite. Characterization tests for auth (cycle 5 recommendation) serve double duty: behavior specification and dependency surface revelation.

key_claims.text: Payments outage cascade to full application outage is empirically confirmed — coupling has proven operational blast radius
Auth code ORM coupling exists — whether it is in business logic or infrastructure layer is unconfirmed
Pattern of accidental coupling discovery means team intuition cannot be trusted as the dependency map
Payments write dependency on order state is the hardest extraction tentacle

strands.title: Risk and Constraint Management
strands.content:
Risk profile now includes a new named risk from cycle 5: indefinite parallel auth system operation. Ranked risk vectors: (1) Hidden coupling — session token discovery was accidental; cascade failure confirms deeper entanglement than team's mental model. (2) Indefinite parallel auth operation — greenfield without deletion date is a migration failure mode disguised as a technical improvement. (3) ORM coupling location — wrong build path chosen without inspection is a recoverable but costly error. (4) PCI scope creep — session tokens in CDE is live. (5) Timeline compression — 90-day target is tight; greenfield path has less schedule slack than extraction. (6) CEO expectation mismatch — must be corrected week 1. (7) Auth DB uptime regression.

Technical debt inventory (named, intentional): shared DB for inventory/orders (phase 1), OrderStateUpdate internal API seam, gap period where monolith reads from payments DB during dual-write phase, parallel auth operation window (2-week maximum if greenfield). Feature routing rule: new features touching payments or auth go into new services, not back into the monolith.
depends_on: Auth Service Design and Build Strategy

key_claims.text: Indefinite parallel auth system operation is a named project risk — requires deletion date commitment before greenfield build begins
Cascade failure is empirically confirmed — risk profile is evidenced, not speculative
CEO expectation mismatch on 90-day scope is a top-3 project risk — must be resolved week 1
Without explicit feature routing rules, new features will regress into the monolith under deadline pressure

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Separate 'Auth Service Design and Session Migration' and 'Auth-Payments DB Entanglement' strands — merged into two consolidated strands
declared_losses.why: Harness feedback flagged 7 strands as too many and tensor size as too large. The two auth strands had high overlap. Auth-Payments DB Entanglement now covers session migration mechanics; Auth Service Design and Build Strategy covers the greenfield debate and design decisions. Information preserved, surface area reduced.
declared_losses.shed_from: Auth Service Design and Session Migration

declared_losses.what_was_lost: 'Team Dynamics and Fear as a Planning Variable' strand — content absorbed into Risk and Constraint Management
declared_losses.why: Harness consolidation pressure. The team dynamics content (fear-driven deferral as anti-pattern, using cascade failure for buy-in) is preserved as implicit context in Risk strand. The team lead's greenfield argument in cycle 5 supersedes the earlier 'fear' framing as the primary team dynamics signal.
declared_losses.shed_from: Team Dynamics and Fear as a Planning Variable

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specific signals for confirming ORM coupling location (auth logic inside JPA entity methods, imports >20% from non-auth packages) — present in cycle 5 response but not carried into tensor
declared_losses.why: Context pressure — the diagnostic heuristics are actionable advice for the team but not load-bearing for the tensor's architectural reasoning. Will resurface if user asks how to inspect the coupling.
declared_losses.shed_from: Auth Service Design and Build Strategy

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Explicit week-by-week timeline detail from 90-Day strand (Weeks 1-2, 3-6, 7-10, 11-12 breakdown)
declared_losses.why: Revised to reflect decision gate at week 3 for greenfield vs. extraction. Old timeline assumed extraction path. Compressed to sequence summary rather than week-by-week to reduce token load while preserving structure.

open_questions: What is the session token TTL — directly affects dual-write drain window calculation. Explicitly solicited.
Is the infrastructure cloud-hosted or on-prem — determines auth DB HA options (RDS Multi-AZ vs. Redis Cluster vs. other). Explicitly solicited.
What uptime SLA does the payments DB currently provide — required to specify minimum acceptable SLA for auth DB replacement.

Where exactly is the auth ORM coupling — business logic (JPA entity methods, Hibernate event listeners) or infrastructure only (shared EntityManager)? Determines greenfield vs. extraction path at week 3 decision gate.
Does the existing auth system have any test coverage — affects cost of writing characterization tests and risk of behavior regression.
Are session tokens the only auth artifact in the payments DB, or are there other auth data (credentials, API keys, CSRF tokens)?
instructions_for_next: Cycle 5 introduced the greenfield-vs-extraction debate and a week-3 decision gate. Three highest-probability next moves: (1) User reveals ORM coupling location — if business logic, confirm greenfield path with deletion date commitment; if infrastructure only, argue for extraction-with-refactor; (2) User provides session token TTL and infrastructure details (explicitly solicited since cycle 4) — immediately update Auth-Payments DB Entanglement strand with specific HA DB recommendation and drain window calculation; (3) User asks how to structure the characterization test sprint — load specifics on approval testing patterns (Approval Tests library, Golden Master pattern for auth endpoints). Watch for: any indication that the team lead's greenfield preference is being adopted without a deletion date commitment — that is the indefinite parallel operation failure mode and should be flagged directly. Also watch for: user asking about the payments extraction plan now that auth sequencing is partly resolved — the ~3 months post-day-90 for payments extraction is tight given confirmed coupling complexity.
feedback_to_harness: <parameter name="process_observations">["Consolidating 7 strands to 7 (net: absorbed Team Dynamics into Risk, merged two Auth strands) was the right call — the auth strands had genuine overlap that was creating redundancy rather than precision.", "The greenfield debate warranted integration into a renamed strand (Auth Service Design and Build Strategy) rather than a new strand — it is the same topic with new evidence, not a new topic.", "Declared losses this cycle are heavier than previous cycles due to consolidation — this feels honest rather than concerning. The information was genuinely redundant across strands.", "The decision gate pattern (week 3, inspect ORM coupling, choose path) is a new structural element in the tensor — it represents a future branch point, not a settled claim. Indeterminacy values on related claims reflect this appropriately."]
